title: Hwy 111, Mecca: Three Stacked Hazard Zones, Position 001 (North)
creator: What the Desert Holds / Google Street View
description: North-facing view from Position 001 at the southern entry point of the Mecca survey corridor (33.565°N, 116.074°W). Three simultaneous hazard zones are legible in a single frame: alkali salt flat to the west, freight railroad running parallel to the highway, and agricultural fields abutting the road to the north and east. No pedestrian infrastructure visible. No sidewalk, no buffer zone between road and adjacent land uses.

title: Hwy 111, Mecca: 'Mecca Arrow' Directional Sign and New Overpass, Position 003 (North)

description: North-facing view from Position 003 (33.571°N, 116.076°W). A directional sign reading 'Mecca →' routes through-traffic past the community rather than through it, making the community visible on the highway only as a destination to avoid. Primary counter-forensic evidence: the highway acknowledges Mecca only to direct drivers elsewhere.

title: Hwy 111, Mecca: Permanent 'No Pedestrian' Sign, Position 004 (East) — PRIMARY EVIDENCE
creator: What the Desert Holds / Google Street View
description: East-facing view from Position 004 (33.574°N, 116.077°W). A permanent state-installed sign reading 'No Pedestrian' is affixed at the primary intersection of the Mecca survey corridor. Primary counter-forensic evidence: the sign does not reflect a temporary condition or construction detour. It formalizes the absence of sidewalks across 1.5 miles of highway through a farmworker community with limited car ownership as a permanent legal prohibition. The sign is a state instrument that converts an infrastructure deficit into a regulatory fact. No equivalent sign appears anywhere in the 1.5-mile Palm Desert survey corridor.

title: Hwy 111, Mecca: Vast Salt Flat and Residential Structures at Edge of Frame, Position 006 (West) — PRIMARY EVIDENCE
creator: What the Desert Holds / Google Street View
description: West-facing view from Position 006 (33.580°N, 116.079°W). A vast white alkali salt flat extends to the horizon, occupying the majority of the visual field. Residential structures of Mecca are barely visible at the right edge of the frame. The salt flat is an active PM2.5 and PM10 source bearing arsenic, DDT, selenium, lead, molybdenum, and pesticide residues — the accumulated chemical record of a century of agricultural and industrial decisions. The landscape does not appear as a hazard: it appears luminous, almost beautiful. The gap between aesthetic appearance and forensic reality is the threshold at which slow violence operates. Primary comparative evidence: paired with hwy_111_palmdesert_001s.jpg. Same highway. Same valley. 30 miles.

title: Hwy 111, Palm Desert: Commercial Corridor Maintained to Western Terminus, Position 007 (West)
creator: What the Desert Holds / Google Street View
description: West-facing view (direction of travel) from Position 007 (33.722°N, 116.384°W). Six-lane divided boulevard with planted median. Multiple active commercial tenants on right side with maintained setbacks and sidewalks. Active multi-lane traffic. The commercial and pedestrian infrastructure of the Palm Desert corridor remains fully intact from eastern entry (Position 001) to western terminus (Position 007). No degradation across the corridor length.

title: Alessandro Drive, Palm Desert: Residential Street with Sidewalks, Position 003 (North)
creator: What the Desert Holds / Google Street View
description: North-facing view from Position 003 on Alessandro Drive, Palm Desert (first street parallel and north of Hwy 111). Residential street with sidewalks on both sides, concrete curbing throughout. Single-family homes with maintained yards, walls, and palms. A residential street sign is readable. Road surface in reasonable condition. Alessandro Drive represents the depth layer of the Palm Desert comparison: even the residential streets immediately behind the highway corridor have continuous sidewalk infrastructure absent entirely from Mecca.

title: Alessandro Drive, Palm Desert: Mixed Commercial Street, Position 003 (West)
creator: What the Desert Holds / Google Street View
description: West-facing view from Position 003 on Alessandro Drive (33.723°N, 116.372°W). Two-lane street with commercial buildings on the left, a 35 mph speed limit sign, utility poles, and a sidewalk visible on the right side. Road surface shows some cracking. This is the most visually comparable image to the Mecca survey conditions in the entire Palm Desert set. And still: sidewalk present, speed limit posted, buildings maintained, no salt flat, no prohibited pedestrian movement.
